reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: All Stacktape config examples correctly use <CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[...]} /> with TypeScript as the source of truth — no YAML blocks, no StacktapeConfig type pattern.
All three DomainConfiguration properties (domainName, customCertificateArn, disableDnsRecordCreation) get their own section with a working code example and clear when-to-use guidance.
The 'Supported resources' table is scannable and gives readers a clear picture of where custom domains apply.
Focus markers and hidden-import pattern match the example page's <CodeBlock> conventions exactly.
FAQ section has exactly 10 Q&A pairs spread across Stacktape-specific, AWS use-case, and choosing/comparison questions — good coverage breadth.
Opening paragraph is self-contained, 40–60 words, and follows the example's definition → reason-to-care shape.
H2s are noun-form and ordered consistently with the example page (When to use → When NOT to use → Basic example → [specific sections] → API Reference → FAQ).
The prerequisite ('run stacktape domain:add once per root domain') is surfaced early in the Basic example section, preventing a common first-timer pitfall.
Frontmatter keys (title, order, seoTitle, seoDescription) match the example page exactly.
problems: The 'Supported resources' table lists web-service, Next.js, Astro, Nuxt, SvelteKit, SolidStart, TanStack, Remix, and static hosting as supporting customDomains, but the only source-grounded type for this is StpDomainAttachableResourceType, which explicitly enumerates only application-load-balancer, http-api-gateway, network-load-balancer, and cdn. The draft does not cite source files for the SSR frontend / static hosting entries, making the table potentially misleading.

The <Tip> callout 'Even with disableDnsRecordCreation: true, you still need a Route53 hosted zone' is not grounded in DomainConfiguration's interface or any provided source file. ACM DNS validation CAN be satisfied by adding the validation CNAME in an external DNS provider (Cloudflare, etc.) without a Route53 hosted zone — the tip may be actively wrong for the external-DNS use case it describes.

The <Info> callout in the 'TLS certificates' section ('For CDN-attached resources, AWS requires the certificate to be in us-east-1') is well-known AWS behavior but is not referenced in any provided source file. The DomainConfiguration interface is silent on this constraint. A source-grounding verifier will flag it as an unsupported Stacktape-specific claim.

The <Tip> about HTTPS being available without custom domains floats between the 'When NOT to use' H2 and 'Supported resources' H2, outside any section. This breaks the visual rhythm — it reads as orphaned content and will confuse readers scanning for where it belongs.

The page has no 'Referenceable parameters' section. The example page includes <ReferenceableParams resource="..." />, and while custom domains is a config concept rather than a standalone resource, some resources supporting customDomains do expose referenceable params (e.g. the URL after domain attachment). The omission is logically defensible, but it creates MDX component palette drift from the example.

The <FeatureComparisonTable> component used in the example page is absent. While there is no obvious head-to-head comparison for this page, minor component palette drift weakens section consistency across the docs site.
mandatoryFixes: Audit the 'Supported resources' table against source files for each listed resource type. The provided source only confirms custom domain support on application-load-balancer, http-api-gateway, network-load-balancer, and cdn via StpDomainAttachableResourceType. For SSR frontends, web-service, and static hosting, either locate and cite the resource type definition confirming customDomains is a property, or narrow the table to the four confirmed types and add a prose note that SSR frameworks may expose custom domains through their underlying load balancer or CDN.

Remove or rewrite the <Tip> in 'External DNS' that claims a Route53 hosted zone is still required when disableDnsRecordCreation: true. This is unsupported by the DomainConfiguration source and may be incorrect for users who can satisfy ACM DNS validation entirely through Cloudflare or another external provider. Replace with a cautious note: 'Stacktape still needs to validate domain ownership to provision the TLS certificate — check ACM documentation for the validation options available with your DNS provider.'

Remove or move the floating <Tip> about HTTPS being available without custom domains. Place it inside the 'When NOT to use' section's body, not as a free-floating block between two H2 sections. Orphaned callouts between H2s break the page's visual rhythm.

Rewrite or remove the <Info> callout about ACM certificates needing us-east-1 for CDN resources if no source file confirms this requirement. If the claim is accurate, add the specific source reference or frame it explicitly as an AWS requirement ('AWS CloudFront requires...') rather than a Stacktape behavior.
optionalImprovements: Add a brief sentence or two on certificate provisioning timing — how long it takes for a custom domain to go live after first deploy. The FAQ partially covers this but new users will look for it in the Basic example section too.

The 'External DNS' section's Tip could be simplified. Currently it tells users to run 'domain:add' even when skipping DNS record creation, which may surprise them. A one-sentence explanation of why (certificate validation) before the code example would reduce the jarring feeling.

Consider adding a small prose callout or sentence in the 'Multiple domains' section noting that each additional certificate provisioning adds deploy time proportionally — useful for teams automating multi-tenant setups.

The FAQ answer for 'How long does it take for a custom domain to start working?' could be more concrete about the difference between first-time setup (minutes to hours for Route53 propagation + certificate validation) vs. subsequent deploys (certificate already exists, near-instant).

reviewerResults.persona: Engineer already shipping with Stacktape, looking up how to configure something correctly under real conditions. Cares about: accuracy of defaults, limits, and behavior under load; whether edge cases and failure modes are mentioned where relevant (scaling, security defaults, cost ceilings, rollback behavior); whether the page would mislead them when they reach production. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices — only flags things that change what they'd configure.
strengths: Comprehensive 'Supported resources' table with direct links gives users fast lookup across the resource types they care about.
Decision support for TLS certificate options (auto-provisioned vs. custom) is well-grounded and opinionated — the 'When to use a custom certificate' note is exactly what the reader needs to decide.
External DNS section pairs a code example with a concrete explanation of when to use it, including the Cloudflare case, which is a real reader segment.
The 'When NOT to use' section has concrete, actionable scenarios (dev stages, internal-only services, prototyping) rather than generic caveats.
All 10 FAQ answers are substantive 2-4 sentence paragraphs; coverage spans Stacktape-specific, AWS-mechanics, and practical-decision questions as required.
Code examples use realistic values (`api.example.com`, `app.example.com`, named resources) and cover all three main paths (basic, custom cert, external DNS, multiple domains).
problems: MDX palette drift: the draft introduces `<Warning>` and `<Info>` callout types that do not appear in the example page, which uses only `<Tip>`. This violates the mandatory palette-matching rule. Both callouts carry informational content that fits `<Tip>` semantics — the one-time `domain:add` note and the us-east-1 certificate note are neither warnings nor info-box-exclusive content.

The 'Supported resources' table lists web services, all seven SSR frontend types, and static hosting as supporting `customDomains`. The source's `StpDomainAttachableResourceType` only enumerates `application-load-balancer`, `http-api-gateway`, `network-load-balancer`, and `cdn`. The extended list is plausible (SSR resources wrap CDN/ALB internally) but is not verified by any provided source file. A wrong claim here will break real user configs.

The `<Tip>` under 'External DNS' asserts: 'you still need a Route53 hosted zone for the domain… Stacktape uses it for DNS-based certificate validation'. The `DomainConfiguration` JSDoc says Stacktape 'won't touch your DNS' — it does not say a Route53 hosted zone is required for cert issuance. ACM DNS validation records can be added to any DNS provider; requiring Route53 for external-DNS users is an additional constraint the source does not state and that could actively mislead Cloudflare-native teams.
mandatoryFixes: MDX palette drift: Replace the `<Warning>` and `<Info>` callout components with `<Tip>` throughout the page to match the example page's callout palette. The domain:add one-time-setup note and the us-east-1 ACM region note are both informational nudges that work as `<Tip>`. Using `<Warning>` and `<Info>` with no precedent in the example section creates callout-type inflation that must be corrected per the palette-matching rule.

Narrow or verify the 'Supported resources' table: the source's `StpDomainAttachableResourceType` is `application-load-balancer | http-api-gateway | network-load-balancer | cdn` only. The table currently adds WebService, NextjsWeb, AstroWeb, NuxtWeb, SvelteKitWeb, SolidStartWeb, TanStackWeb, RemixWeb, and HostingBucket without grounding. Either (a) verify each type's definition file shows a `customDomains` property and cite that source, or (b) remove unverified rows and add a one-line note that SSR and static hosting resources expose `customDomains` via their internal CDN/ALB — but only add that note if source-grounded.

Remove or correct the claim in the External DNS `<Tip>` that 'you still need a Route53 hosted zone for the domain' when `disableDnsRecordCreation: true`. The `DomainConfiguration` source only states Stacktape 'won't touch your DNS'; it does not mandate a hosted zone for external-DNS users. If the hosted zone is truly required for ACM validation in Stacktape's implementation, that constraint must be grounded in a source file. If it is not grounded, remove the sentence rather than state it as fact — it will break the workflow for teams whose DNS authority is entirely in Cloudflare or another provider.
optionalImprovements: The External DNS section would benefit from a concrete sentence on where the user retrieves the endpoint value to create the CNAME/alias — e.g. 'Retrieve the endpoint from `$ResourceParam` after deploy or from `stacktape info:stack`.' The current wording names the mechanism but doesn't show the lookup step.

The FAQ is missing a choosing/comparison question about Route53 vs. external DNS for DNS management with Stacktape. This is a high-intent search query for users who are deciding whether to move DNS to Route53 and would anchor the External DNS section in FAQ discoverability.

The 'Basic example' section could add a one-sentence note about how `stacktape domain:add` verifies nameserver propagation before the first deploy will succeed — first-time users regularly deploy before propagation completes and hit a confusing cert-validation timeout.

Consider adding a short note in the 'TLS certificates' section that auto-provisioned certs for CDN-backed resources are always placed in `us-east-1` automatically by Stacktape — this distinguishes the auto path from the custom-cert path that requires the user to manage region placement, and sets a clear expectation.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages MUST have a 10-question FAQ that mixes (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?', 'How do I run RDS migrations?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?', 'ALB vs API Gateway?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ — it should cover what readers actually search for, not just what's in our types. Penalize FAQs with fewer than 10 questions. Do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps, recipes).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Opening paragraph is self-contained, 57 words, clearly answers 'what is this and why does it matter' — extractable by AI engines.
All three DomainConfiguration properties (domainName, customCertificateArn, disableDnsRecordCreation) each get a dedicated section with a working code example and decision-support prose.
The 'Supported resources' table is highly scannable and cited-friendly — maps resource types to pages and typical use cases in a single view.

Code examples are realistic and copy-paste complete. Each example targets a different resource (HttpApiGateway, ApplicationLoadBalancer) which demonstrates that the customDomains pattern works across resource types.

FAQ spans 10 questions with reasonable coverage across Stacktape-specific, AWS-mechanics, and choosing/comparison categories (Route53 cost, domain propagation, Cloudflare interop, what happens on stack delete).
H2 heading style (noun-form), ordering (When to use → When NOT to use → ... → API Reference → FAQ), and frontmatter keys all match the example page precisely.

Decision support is present in every opt-in section: TLS certificates section explains when to use a custom certificate vs the default; External DNS section explains when external DNS management applies.
problems: MDX component palette drift: the draft introduces a `<Warning>` callout that the example page does not use. The example uses only `<Info>` and `<Tip>`. The Warning content ('The domain:add step is a one-time setup per root domain...') is positive/advisory, not a destructive-consequence warning, making `<Tip>` the semantically correct choice anyway.

The `<Info>` callout in the TLS certificates section claims 'Stacktape handles this automatically for auto-provisioned certificates' regarding the us-east-1 certificate requirement for CDN-attached resources. The DomainConfiguration source files do not mention this behavior. This is an ungrounded Stacktape-specific claim dressed as a sourced fact.

The Tip inside the External DNS section states 'you still need a Route53 hosted zone for the domain' even when disableDnsRecordCreation is true, attributing this to certificate validation. The DomainConfiguration JSDoc for disableDnsRecordCreation says only 'Stacktape will still provision the TLS certificate but won't touch your DNS' — it does not explicitly state a hosted-zone requirement for certificate validation. This claim may be correct operationally but is not grounded in the provided source.

The Basic example section's opening paragraph starts with a prerequisite ('Before attaching a custom domain, your domain must have a Route53 hosted zone...') rather than a self-contained definition of what the basic example demonstrates. This breaks AEO extractability — an AI engine extracting the first paragraph of the 'Basic example' H2 gets a prerequisite, not a snippet about the configuration pattern.
mandatoryFixes: Replace the `<Warning>` component with `<Tip>` to match the example page's MDX component palette. The example page uses only `<Info>` and `<Tip>` callouts — introducing `<Warning>` creates palette drift across the section. The Warning content is advisory (clarifying one-time domain:add setup), which maps naturally to `<Tip>` semantics.
optionalImprovements: Rewrite the opening paragraph of the 'Basic example' H2 to be self-contained. Instead of leading with the Route53 prerequisite, open with a sentence that defines the configuration pattern ('The minimal custom domain configuration is a single `customDomains` entry with a `domainName`...') and then state the prerequisite as a follow-on sentence. This makes the section extractable as a standalone snippet.

Soften or remove the ungrounded claim in the `<Info>` callout about Stacktape auto-handling the us-east-1 certificate requirement for CDN domains. Either verify this against source (e.g., the CDN resolver or domain-attach logic) before publishing, or rephrase to 'AWS requires the certificate to be in us-east-1 for CloudFront — ensure your custom certificate is in that region if providing one for a CDN-attached resource.'

The Tip inside the External DNS section ('you still need a Route53 hosted zone for the domain') is a potentially surprising constraint that deserves more precise wording. If the requirement is confirmed by source, state it as a fact in prose rather than burying it inside a Tip — readers skimming Tip callouts for advisory notes may underweight this as a hard requirement.

The FAQ's choosing/comparison category is underrepresented. Currently only 2 of 10 questions are comparison-style ('Can I use a domain registered outside AWS?', 'Can I use Cloudflare?'). Consider swapping one Stacktape-specific question for a direct comparison such as 'Route53 vs Cloudflare for DNS management — which should I use with Stacktape?'

The 'How long does it take for a custom domain to start working?' FAQ answer claims DNS propagation 'can take up to 48 hours in edge cases'. This is a well-known AWS/internet fact and acceptable in a FAQ, but the answer could be more useful if it distinguished between: (1) time for ACM certificate issuance (typically minutes), (2) Route53 record propagation (typically seconds to minutes for records within Route53's own nameservers), and (3) external DNS TTL propagation if the user is switching from another provider.

issues.statement: The configuration shape is the same across all these resources — once you've set up a custom domain on one resource, the same pattern works everywhere.
issues.reason: While the DomainConfiguration interface is shared, Application Load Balancer and Network Load Balancer accept `DomainConfiguration[] | string[]` (a backward-compatible string array format), whereas other resources accept only `DomainConfiguration[]`. The claim that 'the same pattern works everywhere' slightly overstates uniformity. ALB and NLB have an additional legacy format option.
issues.suggestedFix: Soften to: 'The core configuration uses the same `DomainConfiguration` structure across all these resources.' Optionally note that ALB and NLB also accept a legacy string array format for backward compatibility.

issues.statement: Deleting a stack removes the DNS records and TLS certificate association, but does not delete the Route53 hosted zone or your domain registration. The hosted zone persists for reuse in future stacks.
issues.reason: This is a specific behavioral claim about stack deletion behavior that is not explicitly stated in the provided source files. It's a reasonable inference (domain:add is a separate command, so the hosted zone likely persists), but the source doesn't explicitly confirm this delete-time behavior.
issues.suggestedFix: Add a qualifying phrase like 'The Route53 hosted zone created by `domain:add` is managed independently of any stack and persists after stack deletion.' This frames it as the expected design rather than an observed guarantee.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages MUST have a 10-question FAQ that mixes (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?', 'How do I run RDS migrations?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?', 'ALB vs API Gateway?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ — it should cover what readers actually search for, not just what's in our types. Penalize FAQs with fewer than 10 questions. Do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps, recipes).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.

issues.statement: The core `DomainConfiguration` structure is the same across all resources that accept it.

The draft correctly mentions the legacy string-array format for ALB and NLB one sentence later, but the preceding sentence's blanket claim of identical structure could confuse readers who try passing string arrays to an HTTP API Gateway.

Application Load Balancers and Network Load Balancers additionally accept a legacy `string[]` format for backward compatibility.' — making the scope of the exception immediately clear in context.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is well-grounded overall. The DomainConfiguration type, its three properties, defaults, and JSDoc descriptions are accurately reflected. The four core domain-attachable resource types match the source, and the claim that additional resources (web services, SSR frontends, static hosting) accept customDomains is confirmed by their type definitions. reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages MUST have a 10-question FAQ that mixes (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?', 'How do I run RDS migrations?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?', 'ALB vs API Gateway?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ — it should cover what readers actually search for, not just what's in our types. Penalize FAQs with fewer than 10 questions. Do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps, recipes).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages MUST have a 10-question FAQ that mixes (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?', 'How do I run RDS migrations?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?', 'ALB vs API Gateway?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ — it should cover what readers actually search for, not just what's in our types. Penalize FAQs with fewer than 10 questions. Do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps, recipes).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
